publications.editedComment: A museum card with enough toggles to satisfy a human and almost enough blank space to satisfy a cat. `reactable-card` handles selection and optional reactions. The body shows title, source name, folder, notes, canonical status, discovery and preview badges, art id, ratings, review totals, and optional metadata or debug JSON. Broken, Building, Working, Needs context, Retired, and Preview unsupported each receive their own icon and border treatment. Edit emits an id. Copy writes the component name. Open exhibit selects the record in `componentStore`. Nothing here fixes a Component; it merely presents its condition accurately, which is more restraint than most dashboards manage.

The important footnote is that Add to Cart does not use the cart store. The form previews either a pasted image URL, `/api/media/art/{id}`, or a placeholder; offers Art Print, T-Shirt, Sticker, and Mug; accepts a quantity of at least one; then logs the selected type, image, and quantity and shows an alert. Cancel and Close emits `close`. The visible controls therefore model a print-order choice, but persistence and checkout are not implemented in this Component.¹ The interface is suitable as a prototype only when that boundary remains explicit.

Four objects wait beneath the image: print, shirt, sticker, mug. The selected button changes accent; a custom URL may replace the ArtImage endpoint or placeholder; quantity begins at one. Then Add to Cart whispers its payload into `console.log` and raises a browser alert. No item enters `cartStore`. Cancel and Close emits a single event and leaves the parent to dismiss the room. The unsettling thing is not hidden complexity, but the absence of it: a storefront ritual performed perfectly, stopping just before anything is purchased.

reviewer.name: The Customer Who Buys One of Everything

publications.editedComment: Every item, listed. Image. Notes. Type. Unit price. Quantity. Extended price. The local quantity map starts from the cart’s current items and sends edits to `cartStore.updateQuantity`. Remove deletes one id. Clear Cart appears only when items exist and clears all of them. The total comes from `cartStore.totalPrice`. Proceed to Checkout first requires a user id; without one, it shows a login alert, and with one it calls the store checkout. The empty cart receives its own message. One of everything still has to pass through the same inventory and authentication gates as one of anything.

We see the whole basket at once. Each stored item displays its image, notes, type, price, editable quantity, and computed line total. Quantity changes return to the cart store; Remove and Clear Cart call their matching store actions; the final total is read from the store. Checkout is guarded by `userStore.userId`: guests receive a login alert, while signed-in visitors continue through `cartStore.checkout()`. When no items remain, the empty-state message replaces the controls. Many choices can gather here, but every mutation still has one accountable store path.

Two plans. One membership bit. Free lists public tools, forums, and a nonbinding birthday-jellybean joke. Patron advertises 1000 monthly mana tokens, priority support, private content, and early access at five dollars per month. `isPatron` reads `user.isMember`; it changes the highlighted card and swaps Subscribe for Cancel. Both actions reject a missing user id. Subscribe delegates to `cartStore.subscribe`. Cancel delegates to `cancelSubscription`, checks its success result, sets `isMember` false, and reports completion. Errors occupy a separate field. The limits are visible, even where the promised benefits are only interface copy.

Two big plan cards, one bright state switch! Free is highlighted when `user.isMember` is false; Patron is highlighted when it is true. The Patron button either starts a five-dollar subscription through `cartStore.subscribe()` or cancels through `cartStore.cancelSubscription()`. Both paths require a logged-in user. Successful cancellation flips the local user membership flag and prints a thank-you message; failures print the caught message. The page also displays the advertised Free and Patron benefits, including 1000 mana tokens. Clickable plan theater on top, two compact store calls underneath. Pixel edges visible.

The overlay establishes coverage immediately, preloads the Kind Robots logo, and rotates live-region messages while the application wakes. Two extra messages arrive 1.25 seconds apart. If stores are still unready, another message appears every 1.6 seconds. Once both readiness and the minimum sequence are satisfied, a hold guarantees the configured minimum duration, then `revealDesktop` runs, `hiding` emits, and opacity fades for 650 milliseconds. Transition completion or a fallback timer emits `hidden` exactly once. Intervals and pending fade timers are cleared on unmount. The path from covered to visible has redundant signaling, not a race into darkness.

The weather stays covered until two fronts meet: the minimum message sequence has finished and `storesReady` is true. On mount, the overlay emits `covered`, shows the eager-loaded logo, and begins with a fixed message. Two timed replacements follow; if readiness is late, messages rotate every 1.6 seconds. Readiness schedules a final hold based on elapsed time, stops rotation, reveals the desktop, emits `hiding`, and starts the opacity fade. The opacity transition or fallback timeout emits `hidden` once. Unmount clears every tracked interval and timeout. Forecast: deliberate clearing, with no duplicate all-clear signal.
